audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning a hotel about holding a party there.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Clare House Hotel, Andrew speaking, I'm the Events Manager.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Andrew is the Events Manager, so Events has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Clare House Hotel, Andrew speaking, I'm the Events Manager.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Good morning, Andrew.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: My name is Samantha.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: I'm arranging a party for my parents' 50th wedding anniversary.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: And I'm ringing to ask about hiring a room sometime next September, also my parents and several of the guests will need accommodation.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: OK, I'm sure we can help you with that.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Will you be having a sit down meal or a buffet?
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Probably a sit down.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: And do you know how many people there'll be?
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Around 80, I think.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Well we have two rooms that can hold that number.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: One is the Adelphi room, that can seat 85 or hold over 100 if people are standing for a buffet.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: If you have live music, there's room for four or five musicians in the gallery.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Overlooking the room, our guests usually appreciate the fact that the music can be loud enough for dancing, but not too loud for conversation.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Yes, I really don't like it when you can't talk.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Now the Adelphi Room is at the back of the hotel, and there are French windows.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Leading out onto the terrace.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: This has a beautiful display of pots of roses at that time of the year.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Which direction does it face?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Southwest, so that side of the hotel gets the sun in the afternoon and early evening.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Very nice.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: From the terrace, you can see the area of trees within the grounds of the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Or you can stroll through there to the river.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: That's on the far side, so it isn't visible from the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: OK, then another option is the Carlton room.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: This is a bit bigger.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: It can hold up to 110 people, and it has the advantage of a stage, which is useful if you have any entertainment.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Or indeed a small band can fit onto it.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Hmm. And can you go outside from the room?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: No, the Carlton room is on the first floor.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: But on one side, the windows look out onto the lake.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: I think either of those rooms would be suitable.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Can I tell you about some of the options we offer in addition?
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Please do.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: As well as a meal, you can have an mc, a master of ceremonies, who'll be with you throughout the party.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: What exactly is the MC's function?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: I suppose they make a speech during the meal, if we need one, do they?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: All our MCs are trained as public speakers, so they can easily get people's attention.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Many guests are glad to have someone who can make themselves heard above the chatter, and they're also your support.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: If anything goes wrong, the MC will deal with it, so you can relax.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Great, I'll need to ask you about food, but something else that's important is accommodation.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: You obviously have rooms in the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: But do you also have any other accommodation, like cabins, for example?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Yes, there are 5 in the grounds, all self contained.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: They each sleep 2 to 4 people, and have their own living room, bathroom, and small kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: That sounds perfect for what we'll need.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now you have various facilities, don't you?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Are they all included in the price of hiring the room?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: The pool for instance.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: Normally you'd be able to use it, but it'll be closed throughout September for refurbishment, I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: The gym will be available though, at no extra charge.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: That's open all day from 6 in the morning until midnight.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: And the tennis courts but there is a small additional payment for those.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: We have four courts, and it's worth booking in advance if you possibly can, as there can be quite a long waiting list for them.
audioscript.speaker: ANDREW

audioscript.text: Right, now could we discuss the food?
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: This would be dinner around 7 o'clock...
audioscript.speaker: SAMANTHA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Hotel facilities

test_text.title: An Overview of Activities and Entertainment at the Hotel

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a holiday company representative talking to a group of tourists in a large hotel about things they can do during their holiday.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: I'm Jake Stevens, and I'm your rep here at the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: I'm sure you'll all have a great time here.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: So, let me tell you a bit about what's on offer.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: I'll start by telling you about some of the excursions that are available for guests.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: One thing you have to do while you're here is go dolphin watching.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: On our boat trips we pretty well guarantee you'll see dolphins.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: If you don't, you can repeat the trip free of charge.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: We organize daily trips for just 35 euros.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Unfortunately, there aren't any places left for this afternoon's trip, but come and see me to book for later in the week.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: If you're energetic, I'd recommend our forest walk.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's a guided walk of about 7 km.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: There'll be a stop halfway, and you'll be provided with a drink and sandwiches.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: There's some fairly steep climbs up the hills, so you need to be reasonably fit for this one.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: With good shoes, though, and bring a waterproof in case it rains.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's just 25 euros, all inclusive, and it's every Wednesday.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Then on Thursdays, we organize a cycle trip, which will give you all the fun of biking without the effort.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: We'll take you and your bike up to the top of Mount Larna.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And leave you to bike back.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's a 700 meter drop in just 20 kilometers, so this isn't really for inexperienced cyclists, as you'll be going pretty fast.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And if it's a clear day, you'll have fantastic views.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: On our local craft tour, you can find out about the traditional activities in the island.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And the best thing about this trip is that it's completely free.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: You'll be taken to a factory where jewelry is made, and also a ceramics center.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: If you want, you can buy some of the products, but that's entirely up to you.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: The trip starts after lunch on Thursday, and you'll return by 6:00 pm.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: If you're interested in astronomy, you may already know that the island's one of the best places in the world to observe the night sky.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: We can offer trips to the observatory on Friday for those who are interested.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: They cost €90 per person, and you'll be shown the huge telescopes, and have a talk from an expert who will explain all about how they work.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Afterwards, we'll head down to Sunset Beach, where you can have a dip in the ocean if you want, before we head off back to the hotel.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Finally, there's horse riding.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: This is organized by the Equestrian Center over near Playa Cortino, and it's a great experience if you're a keen horseback rider.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Or even if you've never been on a horse before, they take you down to the beach, and you can canter along the sand and through the waves.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It costs 35 euros.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And it's available every day.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, there's plenty to do in the daytime, but what about night life?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Well, the No. 1 attraction's called 'Musical Favorites'.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Guests enjoy a three-course meal and unlimited free drinks, and watch a fantastic show, starting with a musical set in Paris, and then crossing the Atlantic to Las Vegas, and finally Copacabana.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: At the end, the cast members come down from the stage still in their stunning costumes.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And you'll have a chance to chat with them.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's hugely popular, so let me know now if you're interested, because it's no good leaving it until the last minute.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's on Friday night.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Tickets are just 50 euros each, but for an extra 10 euros, you can have a table right by the stage.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: If you'd like to go back in time, there's the Castle Feast on Saturday evening.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: It's held in a 12th century castle, and you eat in the great courtyard.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: With ladies in long gowns serving your food.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: You're given a whole chicken each, which you eat in the medieval way, using your hands instead of cutlery.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And you're entertained by competitions where the horseback riders attempt to knock one another off their horses.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: Then you can watch the dancers in the ballroom.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: And join in as well if you want.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: OK, so now if anyone has...
audioscript.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: And leave you to bike back.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: It's a 700 meter drop in just 20 kilometers, so this isn't really for inexperienced cyclists, as you'll be going pretty fast.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And if it's a clear day, you'll have fantastic views.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: On our local craft tour, you can find out about the traditional activities in the island.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And the best thing about this trip is that it's completely free.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: You'll be taken to a factory where jewelry is made, and also a ceramics center.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: If you want, you can buy some of the products, but that's entirely up to you.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: The trip starts after lunch on Thursday, and you'll return by 6:00 pm.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: If you're interested in astronomy, you may already know that the island's one of the best places in the world to observe the night sky.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: We can offer trips to the observatory on Friday for those who are interested.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: They cost €90 per person, and you'll be shown the huge telescopes, and have a talk from an expert who will explain all about how they work.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Afterwards, we'll head down to Sunset Beach, where you can have a dip in the ocean if you want, before we head off back to the hotel.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Finally, there's horse riding.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: This is organized by the Equestrian Center over near Playa Cortino, and it's a great experience if you're a keen horseback rider.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Or even if you've never been on a horse before, they take you down to the beach, and you can canter along the sand and through the waves.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: It costs 35 euros.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And it's available every day.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So, there's plenty to do in the daytime, but what about night life?
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Well, the No. 1 attraction's called 'Musical Favorites'.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Guests enjoy a three-course meal and unlimited free drinks, and watch a fantastic show, starting with a musical set in Paris, and then crossing the Atlantic to Las Vegas, and finally Copacabana.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: At the end, the cast members come down from the stage still in their stunning costumes.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And you'll have a chance to chat with them.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: It's hugely popular, so let me know now if you're interested, because it's no good leaving it until the last minute.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: It's on Friday night.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Tickets are just 50 euros each, but for an extra 10 euros, you can have a table right by the stage.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: If you'd like to go back in time, there's the Castle Feast on Saturday evening.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: It's held in a 12th century castle, and you eat in the great courtyard.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: With ladies in long gowns serving your food.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: You're given a whole chicken each, which you eat in the medieval way, using your hands instead of cutlery.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And you're entertained by competitions where the horseback riders attempt to knock one another off their horses.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: Then you can watch the dancers in the ballroom.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: And join in as well if you want.
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: OK, so now if anyone has...
utterances.speaker: JAKE STEVENS

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What information does the speaker give about each of the following excursions?
textReadable: What information does the speaker give about each of the following excursions?

items: dolphin watching <input>
forest walk      <input>
cycle trip       <input>
local craft tour <input>
observatory trip <input>
horse riding     <input>
body.title: Excursions
Which TWO things does the speaker say about the attraction called Musical Favourites?
options: You pay extra for drinks.
You must book it in advance.
You get a reduction if you buy two tickets.
You can meet the performers.
You can take part in the show.

body.title: Which TWO things does the speaker say about the Castle Feast?
options: Visitors can dance after the meal.
There is a choice of food.
Visitors wear historical costume.
Knives and forks are not used.
The entertainment includes horse races.

test_text.title: A Discussion about a Literature for Children Course and Related Books

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear Stephanie, who is thinking about taking a one year course in children's literature, talking to Trevor, who is currently taking the course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25 now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Trevor.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Hello, Stephanie.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: You said you wanted to talk about the course I'm taking on literature for children.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I'm thinking of doing it next year, but I'd like to find out more about it first.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: OK, well as you probably know, it's a one year course.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: It's divided into 6 modules, and you have to take all of them.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: One of the most interesting ones for me at least, was about the purpose of children's literature.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: You mean whether it should just entertain children, or should be educational as well?
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Right, and whether the teaching should be factual, giving them information about the world, or ethical, teaching them values.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: What's fascinating is that the writer isn't necessarily conscious of the message they're conveying. For instance, a story might show a child who has a problem as a result of not doing what an adult has told them to do, implying that children should always obey adults.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: I see what you mean.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: That module made me realize how important stories are.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: They can have a significant effect on children as they grow up.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Actually, it inspired me to have a go at it myself, just for my own interest.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: I know I can't compete with the really popular stories, like the Harry Potter books, they're very good.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: And even young kids like my 7 year old niece love reading them.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm, I'm very interested in illustrations in stories.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Is that covered in the course?
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, there's a module on pictures.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: And how they're sometimes central to the story.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's good.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I remember some frightening ones I saw as a child, and I can still see them vividly in my mind years later.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Pictures can be so powerful, just as powerful as words.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I've always enjoyed drawing, so that's the field I want to go into when I finish the course.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I bet that module will be really helpful.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I'm sure it will.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: We also studied comics in that module, but I'm not convinced of their value, not compared with books.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: One of the great things about words is that you use your imagination, but with a comic you don't have to.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: But children are so used to visual input on TV, video games, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: There are plenty of kids who wouldn't even try to read a book, so, I think comics can serve a really useful purpose.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: You mean it's better to read a comic than not to read at all?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, I suppose you're right.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: I just think it's sad when children don't read books.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: What about books for girls and books for boys?
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Does the course go into that?
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, there's a module on it.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: For years, lots of stories in English at least, assumed that boys went out and did adventurous things, and girls stayed at home and played with dolls.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: I was amazed how many books were targeted at just one sex or the other.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Of course, this reflects society as it is when the books are written.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: That's true.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: So it sounds as though you think it's a good course.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Have you been reading lots of children's stories to help you decide whether to take the course?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, I've gone as far back as the late 17th century, though I know there were earlier children's stories.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: So does that mean you've read Perrault's fairy tales, Cinderella, The Sleeping Beauty, and so on?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Yes, they must be important, because no stories of that type had been written before.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: These were the first.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Then there's the Swiss family Robinson.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I haven't read that.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: The English name makes it sound as though Robinson is the family surname, but a more accurate translation would be the Swiss Robinsons, because it's about a Swiss family who are shipwrecked.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Like Robinson Crusoe in the novel of a century earlier.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Well, I never knew that.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Have you read Hoffmann's The Nutcracker and the Mouse King?
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Wasn't that the basis for Tchaikovsky's ballet The Nutcracker?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: It has some quite bizarre elements.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I hope you've read Oscar Wilde's The Happy Prince.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: It's probably my favorite children's story of all time.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Ah. Mine too.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: And it's so surprising, because Wilde is best known for his plays, and most of them are very witty, but The Happy Prince is really moving.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I struggled with Tolkien's The Lord of the Rings, they are long books, and I gave up after one.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: It's extremely popular though.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: But whereas something like The Happy Prince just carried me along with it, The Lord of the Rings took more effort than I was prepared to give it.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: I didn't find that, I love it.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Another one I've read is War Horse.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Oh yes, it's about the First World War, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Hardly what you'd expect for a children's story.
audioscript.speaker: TREVOR

audioscript.text: Exactly, but it's been very successful.
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: Have you read any...
audioscript.speaker: STEPHANIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What does Trevor find interesting about the purpose of children's literature?
options: the fact that authors may not realise what values they're teaching
the fact that literature can be entertaining and educational at the same time
the fact that adults expect children to imitate characters in literature

items.title: Trevor says the module about the purpose of children's literature made him
options: analyse some of the stories that his niece reads.
wonder how far popularity reflects good quality.
decide to start writing some children's stories.

items.title: Stephanie is interested in the Pictures module because
options: she intends to become an illustrator.
she can remember beautiful illustrations from her childhood.
she believes illustrations are more important than words.

items.title: Trevor and Stephanie agree that comics
options: are inferior to books.
have the potential for being useful.
discourage children from using their imagination.

items.title: With regard to books aimed at only boys or only girls, Trevor was surprised
options: how long the distinction had gone unquestioned.
how few books were aimed at both girls and boys.
how many children enjoyed books intended for the opposite sex.

text: What comment is made about each of these stories?
textReadable: What comment is made about each of these stories?

list: inspired a work in a different area of art
more popular than the author's other works
original title refers to another book
items: Perrault's fairy tales                     <input>
"The Swiss Family Robinson" <input>
"The Nutcracker and The Mouse King" <input>
"The Lord of the Rings" <input>
"War Horse" <input>
body.title: Stories

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a branch of archaeology called Marine Archaeology.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In today's class, I'm going to talk about marine archaeology, the branch of archaeology focusing on human interaction with the sea, lakes and rivers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's the study of ships, cargoes, shipping facilities, and other physical remains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'll give you an example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then go on to show how this type of research is being transformed by the use of the latest technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Atlit-Yam was a village on the coast of the eastern Mediterranean.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which seems to have been thriving until around 7,000 BC, the residents kept cattle, caught fish, and stored grain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They had wells for fresh water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many of their houses were built around a courtyard, and were constructed of stone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The village contained an impressive monument, seven half-tonne stones standing in a semicircle around a spring, that might have been used for ceremonial purposes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Atlit-Yam may have been destroyed swiftly by a tsunami, or climate change may have caused glaciers to melt, and sea levels to rise.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Flooding the village gradually.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Whatever the cause, it now lies 10 meters below the surface of the Mediterranean, buried under sand at the bottom of the sea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's been described as the largest and best preserved prehistoric settlement ever found on the seabed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For marine archaeologists, Atlit-Yam is a treasure trove.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Research on the buildings, tools, and the human remains has revealed how the bustling village once functioned.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And even what diseases some of its residents suffered from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But of course, this is only one small village, one window into a lost world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For a fuller picture, researchers need more sunken settlements.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the hard part is finding them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Underwater research used to require divers to find shipwrecks or artifacts, but in the second half of the 20th century, various types of underwater vehicles were developed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some controlled from a ship on the surface, and some of them autonomous, which means they don't need to be operated by a person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Autonomous underwater vehicles, or AUVs, are used in the oil industry, for instance, to create maps of the seabed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before rigs and pipelines are installed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To navigate, they use sensors such as compasses and sonar.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Until relatively recently, they were very expensive, and so heavy that they had to be launched from a large vessel with a winch.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the latest AUVs are much easier to maneuver.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can be launched from the shore or a small ship. And they're much cheaper, which makes them more accessible to research teams.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They are also very sophisticated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can communicate with each other.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And for example, work out the most efficient way to survey a site, or to find particular objects on the seabed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Field tests show the approach can work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, in a trial in 2015 three AUVs searched for wrecks at Marzamemi, off the coast of Sicily.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The site is the final resting place of an ancient Roman ship, which sank in the 6th century AD.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While ferrying prefabricated marble elements for the construction of an early church, the AUVs mapped the area in detail.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finding other ships carrying columns of the same material.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Creating an internet in the sea for AUVs to communicate is no easy matter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Wi-Fi networks on land use electromagnetic waves, but in water, these will only travel a few centimeters.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, a more complex mix of technologies is required.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For short distances, AUVs can share data using light, while acoustic waves are used to communicate over long distances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But more creative solutions are also being developed, where an AUV working on the seabed offloads data to a second AUV, which then surfaces and beams the data home to the research team using a satellite.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's also a system that enables AUVs to share information from seabed scans and other data.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, if an AUV surveying the seabed finds an intriguing object, it can share the coordinates of the object, that is its position with a nearby AUV that carries superior cameras.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And arrange for that AUV to make a closer inspection of the object.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Marine archaeologists are excited about the huge potential of these AUVs for their discipline.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One site where they're going to be deployed is the Gulf of Baratti, off the Italian coast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In 1974, a 2,000-year-old Roman vessel was discovered here, in 18 meters of water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When it sank, it was carrying medical goods in wooden or tin receptacles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Its cargo gives us insight into the treatments available all those years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Including tablets that are thought to have been dissolved to form a cleansing liquid for the eyes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Other Roman ships went down nearby, taking their cargoes with them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some held huge pots made of terracotta.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some were used for transporting cargoes of olive oil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And others held wine.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In many cases, it's only these containers that remain, while the wooden ships have been buried under silt on the seabed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another project that's about to be...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a branch of archaeology called Marine Archaeology.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In today's class, I'm going to talk about marine archaeology, the branch of archaeology focusing on human interaction with the sea, lakes and rivers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's the study of ships, cargoes, shipping facilities, and other physical remains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'll give you an example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then go on to show how this type of research is being transformed by the use of the latest technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Atlit-Yam was a village on the coast of the eastern Mediterranean.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which seems to have been thriving until around 7,000 BC, the residents kept cattle, caught fish, and stored grain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They had wells for fresh water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many of their houses were built around a courtyard, and were constructed of stone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The village contained an impressive monument, seven half-tonne stones standing in a semicircle around a spring, that might have been used for ceremonial purposes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Atlit-Yam may have been destroyed swiftly by a tsunami, or climate change may have caused glaciers to melt, and sea levels to rise.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Flooding the village gradually.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Whatever the cause, it now lies 10 meters below the surface of the Mediterranean, buried under sand at the bottom of the sea.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's been described as the largest and best preserved prehistoric settlement ever found on the seabed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For marine archaeologists, Atlit-Yam is a treasure trove.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Research on the buildings, tools, and the human remains has revealed how the bustling village once functioned.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And even what diseases some of its residents suffered from.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But of course, this is only one small village, one window into a lost world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For a fuller picture, researchers need more sunken settlements.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the hard part is finding them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

body.title: The hunt for sunken settlements and ancient shipwrecks
